title: Ada and the Great Migration
synopsis: In 1934 Kansas, young Ada discovers an injured sandhill crane during the Dust Bowl and embarks on an adventure to help it rejoin the great migration, learning about friendship, courage, and the power of working together.

chapters.title: The Dust Storm
chapters.content:
Ada Mae Johnson pressed her nose against the kitchen window and watched the brown cloud roll across the prairie. It looked like a giant monster swallowing up everything in its path.

"Another dust storm coming," she whispered to herself. The year was 1934, and these storms had been happening a lot lately in Kansas.

"Ada! Get away from that window!" called her mama from the kitchen. "Help me cover the food before the dust gets in."

Ada ran to help her mama, Rosa, who was quickly putting cloth over the bread and milk. Her papa, Samuel, came in from the barn, shaking dust from his hat.

"This one looks real bad," Papa said, his dark face worried. "We better get to the root cellar."

The wind started howling like a wild animal. Ada grabbed her little cloth doll and followed her parents down into the underground cellar. It was dark and smelled like potatoes and earth.

"Tell me about the birds again, Mama," Ada said as dust rattled against the cellar door above them.

Mama smiled and put her arm around Ada. "Well, honey, every spring and fall, millions of birds fly right over our farm. They're going south for winter or north for summer. It's called migration."

"Do they get scared of the dust storms?" Ada asked.

"I reckon they do," Papa said. "But they keep flying anyway. Birds are brave like that."

The storm howled for what felt like hours. When it finally got quiet, they climbed back up to the house. Everything was covered in a thick layer of brown dust.

"Look!" Ada pointed out the window. "The sky is clear now!"

That's when she saw something gray and white in their yard, not moving at all.

illustrations.description: A massive brown dust storm approaches a small Kansas farmhouse in 1934, with golden sunlight filtering through the edges of the dark cloud. Ada, a young girl with curly brown hair, presses her face against a window while her parents move around the kitchen behind her. The scene shows the contrast between the warm interior and the threatening storm outside.
illustrations.alt: Ada watching a dust storm approach through the farmhouse window

chapters.title: The Injured Crane
chapters.content:
"Papa, there's something in our yard!" Ada called out.

Papa squinted through the dusty window. "Looks like a big bird. Might be hurt."

They all went outside, covering their mouths with cloths because dust was still in the air. There in their yard was the biggest bird Ada had ever seen. It was gray with a red patch on its head and stood almost as tall as Ada herself.

"It's a sandhill crane," Papa said quietly. "Don't get too close, Ada. Wild animals can be dangerous when they're scared."

But Ada could see that one of the bird's wings was hanging funny. The crane looked at them with bright yellow eyes, but it didn't try to fly away.

"Oh, Papa, it's hurt!" Ada said. "We have to help it!"

"I don't know, honey," Mama said. "We don't know how to fix a bird's wing."

Just then, their neighbor Miguel rode up on his horse. Miguel was Ada's friend even though he was two years older. His family had moved from Mexico to work on the railroad.

"Hola, Ada!" Miguel called out, then stopped when he saw the crane. "Ay, Dios mío! That's a big bird!"

"It's hurt, Miguel," Ada said. "The dust storm must have knocked it down."

Miguel got off his horse and walked slowly toward the crane. "My abuela knows about hurt animals," he said. "She helped fix a coyote's leg once."

"Could she help our crane?" Ada asked hopefully.

"Maybe," Miguel said. "But first we need to catch it without making the wing worse."

The crane made a low, trumpeting sound. It sounded sad and scared.

"Don't worry, big bird," Ada whispered. "We're going to help you."

illustrations.description: A large sandhill crane with a drooping wing stands in a dusty farmyard, its red-crowned head held high and yellow eyes alert. Ada, a young African American girl, approaches cautiously while her parents watch from behind. Miguel, a Latino boy on horseback, has just arrived. Golden afternoon light cuts through the settling dust, creating a warm but tense atmosphere.
illustrations.alt: The injured sandhill crane in the farmyard with Ada, her parents, and Miguel

chapters.title: Getting Help
chapters.content:
Miguel's grandmother, Esperanza, was the wisest person Ada knew. She had silver hair braided down her back and always smelled like the herbs she grew in her garden.

"Abuela," Miguel said in Spanish, then switched to English so Ada could understand. "There's a hurt crane at Ada's farm. Can you help?"

Esperanza's dark eyes sparkled. "Ah, a sandhill crane! They are sacred birds, you know. Very smart, very loyal. Show me this bird."

They all walked back to Ada's farm. The crane was still in the same spot, but it had tucked its hurt wing close to its body.

"Hmm," Esperanza said, walking slowly around the bird. "Not broken, I think. Maybe just... how do you say... sprained? Like when you twist your ankle."

"Can we fix it?" Ada asked.

"We can try," Esperanza said. "But first, we need to make the bird trust us. Miguel, go get some corn from your papa's barn. Ada, bring water in a shallow pan."

While they gathered supplies, Esperanza told them about sandhill cranes. "They mate for life and live in big groups called flocks. This time of year, they should be flying south for winter."

"Flying south?" Ada's heart sank. "You mean it's supposed to leave?"

"Sí, mija. The migration has probably already started. This poor bird is missing its family."

Ada looked at the crane, who was now drinking from the water pan. "Then we have to make it better fast!"

"These things take time," Esperanza said gently. "But don't worry. Cranes are strong. This one will fly again."

The crane ate some of the corn Miguel had brought. It seemed to be getting more comfortable with them.

"What should we name it?" Ada asked.

"How about Storm?" Miguel suggested. "Since we found it after the dust storm."

"Storm," Ada repeated. "I like that. Hello, Storm. We're going to take good care of you."

illustrations.description: Esperanza, an elderly Mexican woman with long silver braids, kneels near the crane while Ada and Miguel watch. The crane is drinking from a shallow water pan, its posture more relaxed. The farm setting shows a small garden with herbs in the background, and warm golden light filters through the dusty air, creating a peaceful healing atmosphere.
illustrations.alt: Esperanza examining the crane while Ada and Miguel watch with supplies

chapters.title: Caring for Storm
chapters.content:
For the next three days, Ada spent every moment she could with Storm. She brought the crane fresh water and corn kernels. She even tried talking to it.

"My mama says birds are smart," Ada told Storm one morning. "Do you understand me?"

Storm tilted its head and made a soft trilling sound.

"I think that means yes!" Ada giggled.

Miguel came by every day after helping his papa with the railroad work. "Look, Ada," he said on the third day. "Storm is using that wing a little bit."

It was true. Storm was stretching its hurt wing out a tiny bit, though it still couldn't lift it very high.

"Esperanza says we need to help it exercise," Miguel continued. "Like when people hurt their arms and have to move them slowly to get strong again."

They spent the afternoon encouraging Storm to stretch and flap. The big bird seemed to understand what they were trying to do.

"Good job, Storm!" Ada cheered when the crane managed to lift both wings for just a second.

That evening, Papa sat with Ada on the porch. "You know, honey, when Storm gets better, it's going to want to fly south with the other cranes."

"I know," Ada said quietly. "But I wish it could stay. It's like having a best friend who's almost as tall as me."

Papa chuckled. "Wild things belong in the wild, Ada Mae. But taking care of Storm, helping it get strong again - that's a real special thing you're doing."

"Miguel's helping too," Ada said. "And Esperanza taught us what to do."

"That's right. Sometimes the best thing we can do for something we love is help it be free."

That night, Ada dreamed about flying high above the prairie with Storm, seeing the whole world spread out below like a patchwork quilt.

illustrations.description: Ada sits cross-legged in the dust talking to Storm, who stands nearby with its head tilted attentively. Miguel approaches in the background carrying a bucket. The crane is stretching one wing slightly, showing improvement. Warm morning sunlight creates long shadows, and small details like corn scattered on the ground and a water dish show the care being given.
illustrations.alt: Ada talking to Storm while the crane stretches its healing wing

chapters.title: The Other Cranes Arrive
chapters.content:
Ada woke up to the strangest sound she'd ever heard. It was like music, but wild and free. She ran outside in her nightgown and bare feet.

"Mama! Papa! Come quick!" she hollered.

The sky was full of birds. Hundreds and hundreds of sandhill cranes were flying overhead, calling to each other in their trumpeting voices. And Storm was calling back, stretching its neck high and making the loudest sounds Ada had heard from it yet.

"The migration!" Mama gasped as she and Papa rushed outside.

Miguel came running across the field with Esperanza close behind. "Ada! Look at all of them!"

The cranes were starting to land in the fields around their farm. Soon there were gray birds everywhere, walking around and calling to each other.

"They're looking for Storm!" Ada realized. "They came back for their friend!"

Esperanza nodded. "Cranes are very loyal. They don't leave family behind if they can help it."

Storm was getting very excited, trying to walk toward the other cranes. But when it tried to fly, it could only get a few feet off the ground.

"Its wing isn't strong enough yet," Miguel observed.

Some of the other cranes walked closer to Storm. They were making soft, gentle sounds. It looked like they were having a conversation.

"What are they saying?" Ada wondered.

"Probably asking if Storm is okay," Esperanza said. "And Storm is probably telling them about the nice children who helped."

Two of the cranes - bigger ones who might have been Storm's parents - came right up to the fence. They looked at Ada and Miguel, then back at Storm.

"I think they're saying thank you," Ada whispered.

But as the day went on, Ada started to worry. The other cranes were getting restless. They needed to continue their journey south before winter came. What if they couldn't wait for Storm to get better?

illustrations.description: Hundreds of sandhill cranes fill the sky and farmland around Ada's house, creating a spectacular scene of migration. Ada stands in her nightgown pointing upward while Storm calls back to the flock from the yard. The scene is bathed in early morning golden light with cranes landing and walking through the prairie grass, their gray forms creating patterns across the landscape.
illustrations.alt: The great migration arrives with hundreds of cranes filling the sky and fields

chapters.title: A Big Problem
chapters.content:
By the next morning, half the cranes had flown away. The ones that stayed were walking around nervously, flapping their wings like they wanted to leave too.

"They can't wait much longer," Esperanza said sadly. "Winter is coming. If they don't go south soon, they might get caught in snow and ice."

Ada felt tears stinging her eyes. "But Storm still can't fly far enough!"

"Maybe we can help it catch up to the others somehow," Miguel suggested.

"How?" Ada asked. "We can't fly."

That's when Papa had an idea. "What about Mr. Peterson's truck? He hauls grain to the next county. Maybe he could give Storm a ride partway."

Mr. Peterson was their neighbor who owned the biggest farm in the area. He was a tall, quiet man with kind blue eyes.

When they asked him, Mr. Peterson scratched his gray beard. "Well, I've never had a crane for a passenger before," he said. "But I reckon we could try. I'm driving south to Dodge City tomorrow with a load of wheat. That's about fifty miles closer to where those birds are heading."

"Would Storm ride in a truck?" Miguel wondered.

"Only one way to find out," Ada said.

They spent the afternoon getting Storm used to being around the truck. At first, the big bird was scared of the noisy engine. But gradually, it got braver.

"Look!" Ada pointed. "Storm stepped on the running board!"

By evening, they had Storm hopping up into the truck bed, which Papa had lined with soft hay.

"Tomorrow we'll see if this crazy plan works," Papa said.

That night, Ada couldn't sleep. What if Storm got scared and hurt itself worse? What if the plan didn't work? What if she never saw her feathered friend again?

illustrations.description: Mr. Peterson, a tall farmer with a gray beard, stands beside his old grain truck while Storm cautiously approaches. Ada and Miguel watch hopefully as the crane steps onto the truck's running board. The truck bed is lined with golden hay, and remaining cranes can be seen in the background fields, creating tension about the approaching deadline.
illustrations.alt: Storm learning to get comfortable with Mr. Peterson's truck

chapters.title: The Journey Begins
chapters.content:
The next morning came too soon. Ada had barely eaten her breakfast when she heard Mr. Peterson's truck rumbling up their dirt road.

"You ready for this, Storm?" Ada asked the crane.

Storm made a soft sound and let Ada pet its neck feathers. The remaining cranes in the field were calling loudly, as if they knew something important was about to happen.

"I wish I could come with you," Ada said. "But Papa says kids can't miss school, even for crane migrations."

Miguel helped them load Storm into the truck bed. The crane settled into the hay and looked around with bright, curious eyes.

"Don't be scared," Miguel told Storm. "Mr. Peterson is a good driver."

Esperanza came over with a small cloth bag. "Corn for the journey," she explained. "And some herbs that will keep Storm calm."

As the truck started to pull away, something amazing happened. The remaining cranes in the field took off all at once, forming a big V-shape in the sky. They were following the truck!

"Look!" Ada shouted, pointing up. "They're going with Storm!"

The truck drove slowly down the dusty road with the cranes flying overhead. Ada, Miguel, and their families ran alongside for as far as they could.

"Goodbye, Storm!" Ada called out, waving her arms. "Fly safe!"

Storm stood up in the truck bed and called back to her, a long, beautiful trumpet sound that seemed to say "thank you" and "goodbye" all at once.

Ada watched until the truck disappeared over a hill, the cranes still flying above it like a gray cloud.

"Do you think Storm will be okay?" she asked Esperanza.

"I think Storm will do just fine," Esperanza said, putting her arm around Ada. "Thanks to you and Miguel."

illustrations.description: The dramatic moment as Mr. Peterson's truck drives away with Storm in the hay-lined bed, while a V-formation of cranes flies overhead following the truck. Ada and Miguel run alongside the dusty road waving goodbye, with their families watching. Golden morning light illuminates the scene, dust swirls behind the truck, and the vast Kansas prairie stretches to the horizon.
illustrations.alt: The truck departing with Storm while cranes fly overhead and Ada waves goodbye

chapters.title: Waiting for News
chapters.content:
The next few days felt longer than a whole month. Ada kept looking up at the sky, hoping to see Storm flying back to say hello. But the sky stayed empty of cranes.

"No news is good news," Mama told her. "If something bad had happened, Mr. Peterson would have come back to tell us."

Ada tried to focus on her schoolwork, but her teacher, Miss Chen, noticed she was distracted.

"Are you feeling alright, Ada?" Miss Chen asked after Ada stared out the window for the third time during arithmetic.

"I'm waiting to hear about a crane," Ada explained, and told Miss Chen the whole story.

"What an adventure!" Miss Chen said. "Would you like to share it with the class? We've been learning about animal migrations."

So Ada stood in front of all her classmates - including her friend Sarah, who lived in town, and Tommy, whose family had just moved to Kansas from Oklahoma - and told them about Storm.

"Wow," said Sarah. "You actually touched a real wild crane?"

"It was as tall as me," Ada said proudly. "And it learned to trust us."

Tommy raised his hand. "My grandpa in Oklahoma says cranes are good luck. Maybe Storm will bring you good luck for helping it."

That afternoon, Miguel met Ada at her farm with exciting news.

"Mr. Peterson is back!" he announced, out of breath from running. "He's at our house talking to my papa and abuela!"

Ada's heart started beating fast. "Did he say anything about Storm?"

"Come find out!" Miguel grinned.

They ran across the prairie faster than Ada had ever run before. She had to know what happened to Storm. Had their plan worked? Was Storm okay?

illustrations.description: Ada stands at the front of her one-room schoolhouse, gesturing enthusiastically as she tells her classmates about Storm. Miss Chen, a young Asian-American teacher, watches proudly from beside the blackboard. The diverse group of students - including Sarah, Tommy, and others - listen with wide-eyed attention. Warm afternoon sunlight streams through tall windows, illuminating maps and nature drawings on the walls.
illustrations.alt: Ada telling her classmates about Storm's story in the schoolhouse

chapters.title: Good News!
chapters.content:
Mr. Peterson was sitting at Miguel's kitchen table with a big smile on his weathered face. When he saw Ada run in, his smile got even bigger.

"Well, if it isn't Storm's best friend!" he said. "I've got good news, Ada."

Ada sat down so fast she almost missed the chair. "Tell me everything!"

"Well," Mr. Peterson began, "Storm rode in my truck like it had been doing it all its life. Wasn't scared at all after the first few miles. And those other cranes followed us the whole way, calling back and forth to Storm."

"What happened when you got to Dodge City?" Miguel asked.

"That's the best part," Mr. Peterson continued. "When I stopped the truck, Storm hopped right out and started flapping its wings. And you know what? It flew! Not far, mind you, but it flew about fifty yards to where the other cranes had landed."

Ada clapped her hands together. "Storm could fly again!"

"Sure could. And the reunion!" Mr. Peterson chuckled. "Those cranes were making such a racket, dancing around Storm, touching it with their beaks. It was like a family celebration."

Esperanza poured Mr. Peterson more coffee. "Did they continue south?"

"They stayed the night in a field near town. But this morning, when I was getting ready to drive back, I saw them all taking off together. Storm was right there in the middle of the group, flying strong."

Ada felt like her heart might burst with happiness. "Storm is really okay?"

"More than okay," Mr. Peterson said. "That bird is free and back where it belongs. Thanks to you kids caring enough to help."

Miguel's papa clapped Ada on the shoulder. "You did a good thing, mija."

"We all did it together," Ada said, looking around at everyone who had helped save Storm.

illustrations.description: The warm kitchen scene with everyone gathered around Miguel's family table as Mr. Peterson shares his story. Ada sits on the edge of her chair with an expression of joy and relief, while Miguel, his family, and Esperanza listen with satisfied smiles. Coffee cups and a simple meal on the table create a cozy atmosphere, with warm light from the window highlighting the happy faces.
illustrations.alt: Mr. Peterson sharing the good news about Storm's successful release

chapters.title: Spring Returns
chapters.content:
Winter came and went on the Kansas prairie. The dust storms got better when spring arrived and brought rain. Ada and Miguel helped their families plant new crops in the dark, rich soil.

"Do you think Storm will come back this way?" Ada asked one April morning as she scattered corn for their chickens.

"Maybe," Miguel said. "Esperanza says cranes remember places where good things happened."

Ada had been thinking about Storm all winter. She wondered if the crane was warm and safe in the south, if it had made new friends, if it remembered the two children who had helped it.

Then one morning in early May, Ada heard it. That wild, beautiful trumpeting call that she remembered so well.

"Mama!" she yelled, running outside without her shoes. "The cranes are coming back!"

Sure enough, a large flock of sandhill cranes was flying over their farm, heading north for the summer. Ada waved at them and called out, "Hello, cranes!"

Most of the birds kept flying, but one crane broke away from the group and circled back. It landed in their yard, in the exact same spot where they had found Storm almost a year ago.

The crane was bigger now, and its feathers gleamed in the morning sun. It looked at Ada with those familiar bright yellow eyes and made a soft, gentle sound.

"Storm?" Ada whispered. "Is that really you?"

The crane walked closer and bent its long neck down toward Ada. Very carefully, it touched her hand with the tip of its beak, just like a gentle kiss.

"It is you!" Ada laughed, tears of joy running down her cheeks.

Miguel came running from his house. "Ada! Is that Storm?"

"It's Storm!" Ada called back. "Storm came home!"

The crane stayed for just a few minutes, walking around the yard and calling softly to Ada and Miguel. Then it spread its powerful wings - both wings working perfectly now - and took off to rejoin its flock.

As Storm flew away, it called back one more time, a sound that seemed to say "thank you for everything."

Ada and Miguel waved until the cranes disappeared into the blue spring sky.

"We'll see you next year, Storm!" Ada called out. "We'll be here waiting!"

And she knew, somehow, that Storm would remember the way back to the farm where two children had cared enough to help a hurt bird find its way home to the sky.

illustrations.description: The emotional reunion scene as Storm lands in the farmyard, now fully grown and healthy, gently touching Ada's outstretched hand with its beak. Miguel runs toward them in the background while Ada kneels with tears of joy on her face. The spring morning is bright and clear, with green grass growing and the crane's flock visible as distant specks in the blue sky above.
illustrations.alt: Storm's return visit, touching Ada's hand gently in the farmyard

metadata.illustrationStyle: Warm, inviting adventure illustration style. Earthy tones, golden light. Detailed environments with hidden details kids can discover.

questions.question: What year does the story take place?
questions.explanation: The story takes place in 1934 during the Dust Bowl era in Kansas, when dust storms were very common.

questions.question: What kind of bird is Storm?
questions.explanation: Storm is a sandhill crane, a large gray bird with a red patch on its head that migrates seasonally.

questions.question: Who is Miguel's grandmother?
questions.explanation: Miguel's grandmother is Esperanza, who knows a lot about helping hurt animals and teaches them how to care for Storm.

questions.question: How do they help Storm travel south when its wing isn't fully healed?
choices: They wait until it can fly
questions.explanation: Mr. Peterson offers to give Storm a ride partway south in his grain truck, helping it catch up with the other cranes.

questions.question: What does Storm do when it sees Ada again in the spring?
choices: Touches her hand with its beak
questions.explanation: When Storm returns in the spring, it gently touches Ada's hand with its beak, like giving her a gentle kiss to say thank you.

questions.question: Why can't the cranes wait too long for Storm?
questions.explanation: The cranes need to fly south before winter comes, or they might get caught in snow and ice, which would be dangerous for them.

questions.question: What do sandhill cranes eat?
questions.explanation: In the story, Ada and Miguel feed Storm corn kernels, which sandhill cranes enjoy along with other foods like insects and plants.

pages.title: Storm's Journey
pages.instructions: Fill in the blanks to complete the story of Storm's adventure!
pages.content: Ada found an injured [0] in her yard after a [1] storm. The bird's [2] was hurt and it couldn't [3] with the other birds going [4] for winter. Ada and her friend [5] got help from Miguel's [6] who knew about healing animals. They named the bird [7] and took care of it until Mr. [8] could give it a ride in his [9]. In the spring, Storm came [10] to visit Ada and Miguel.

pages.title: Migration Words
pages.instructions: Complete these sentences about bird migration using the word bank!
pages.content: Every year, birds travel long distances in something called [0]. They fly [1] in winter to stay warm and [2] in summer to have babies. Birds like sandhill [3] fly in groups called [4]. They are very [5] to their families and don't like to leave anyone behind. The journey can be [6] because of storms and other dangers.
